sentences.text: We all came out naked in the-- in the-- on that platz, what do you call?
And there was a German woman, you know, a rotten-- [LAUGHS] you know, and she was the selector.
And I wasn't that-- that skinny.
I was never skinny, you know, so I went to the right.
And I hear how she stops her.
You old thing, away with you.
And at this point, this second, split of a second, somebody tapped her.
A Nazi came and tapped her on the back with-- with-- with some papers and she turned.
She didn't go, because she didn't understand-- I don't know what happened.
There was one guy, a Nazi, sitting up there, standing.
And he said to her, can you work?
She said, of course, of course, and sent her over.
Two-- my mother had two miracles, two miracles.
And-- and she made it with me.
So people her age did not make it.
They did not make it really.
And what he did-- I-- I said she must have reminded him of an aunt, of a mother, of somebody to do something like that.
I-- I just could never-- I'll never forget it.
Anyway, so we were selected to go to Bergen-Belsen.
They took a bunch of us to Bergen-Belsen.
And gave us a little better clothes, kind of, better than-- than there, you know.
It was just other people's clothes.
I think that was a uniform, yes.
And they never did-- they never did brand you.
They never did give you a number.
Too many people were coming in.
This was already almost the end of Auschwitz too, you know, the end of the year.
We walked, I think, not-- not too much.
The bad walking was when we arrived to Bergen-Belsen.
This is what I-- and I didn't eat.
The smell, it could be the smell of these burned bodies or fear, you know.
I begged for water, but I couldn't get water.

So when we arrived to Bergen-Belsen, and they told us to get out and the Nazis came with those big, big dogs, with the wolfs, and they asked us, who cannot walk, is sick, should tell him so they're gonna put us on a-- on a bus.
I was afraid to say it.
And if I would say, it would be good, because this was not a bad place.
This was not, at that time, at that time.
So my mother and my friends were holding me and helping me.
We had to walk a few miles, quite a few miles to it.
And we arrived there, and we lived in tents, tents and straw.
And-- and we weren't treated badly.
It wasn't-- it wasn't too bad over there.
We were given nice meals, nice.
They wanted us strong to send out to work, so they didn't want weak and sick people.
Were you feeling-- could you eat there?
My appetite came back, and I could eat, yeah.
And I got back to myself.
And-- and then-- and then we stayed there till they started to build barracks for all the other Auschwitz people, when-- when-- when Bergen-Belsen became hell.
When we left, you know, I mean, every few weeks another group-- how they remembered, because their-- their organization is so great, the Germans.
They knew exactly who came to be sent out, because so many people arrived there, you know?
And I-- we were the last group to go, me and my mother and a few other people.
August, September, October-- October, or half of November or the end of October.
As I said, it-- it was-- but we were told then that they want us to be strong because they need us for work.
But by the time people were coming in from Auschwitz, other people, but this was not the selected group anymore.
The other people stayed over there till they all died and Bergen-Belsen was a horrible place, horrible.
You know, so many people died out and from sicknesses and diseases.
And-- but we were the lucky ones to get out, me and my mother and a few others.
On a-- on a-- on a train.
And we were going and going.
And-- and-- and there were air attacks, air attacks.
And we thought that's the end too.
I mean, it really looked like the end, because everything was shaking, you know, and all the smoke and everything.
Some people probably got killed, you know.
It was a place called Geislingen.
It's a small city, and they had an ammunition factory there.
And so we were taken to work over there.
It was a special camp, also specially made camp.
We had to clean it, to get up early in the morning to make our beds and keep clean.
And-- but the work was horrible, horrible.
We had to sit night-- I worked nights, for 12 hours.
I had to sit at that machine, a boring machine, and make holes in the guns, in the guns so-- and-- what do you call, nail-- a nail could come in there.
And a Nazi woman sat and watched us all night, no food, cold.
I mean, the place was big because they were driving there with-- with trucks.
And no sleep and no food.
And my-- sleep to me was everything.
If I couldn't sleep, then I couldn't function.
I-- I felt there that I am not going to make it.
I really felt these nights and the walking home and standing on appels, you know, 5:00 in the morning, you know, for hours.
And they told us get up and make the beds and get up out of an appel.
There was no sleep, not much sleep at all.
And the pain was so terrible, you know.
Oh, it-- it was bad, anyway.
But this was already towards the end of the war, because I was there from, let's see, November till April.
We had those Holland, Holland shoes.
But just that-- we had only those-- those prisoners' gowns-- you know, clothes.
She was a strong woman, very strong.
She survived because she-- she-- she buried sisters with the grippe that it was at, you know, at time that everybody died, and-- and a mother and a brother.
And I mean, she was a very strong woman.
I mean, she-- to her, I was the apple of her eye.
But she was a domineering woman, very domineering.
And she was with me all the time, because I couldn't-- I couldn't do any other way.
She was just too devoted, too good.
And I loved her, but she was hard to live with, hard to live with.
And so when you were together in the barracks, in the work-- working, and the marches and everything, you would just huddle together and hold each other?
She worked in the daytime, yeah.
But she came all the time to tell me how we're gonna die.
We're not going to make it.
I mean, each time, we're not going to make-- very, very pessimistic, fear, fear.
I was the strong-- I was stronger, yeah.
I-- I didn't give up hope.
I really felt-- and then I said to myself, look.
I-- I was always afraid of dying as a kid.
As a child, fear of dying always, always fear, even older.
And at that time, there was no fear of dying, absolutely not.
And I said to myself, well, look, you're gonna learn the mystery of life, the mystery.
This is how I went with, with positive, like positive.
They just made shifts, different shifts.
And so Mother had it a little better, you know, than I, because she could, you know, sleep a little.
Well, not-- I don't think so.
You know, we were there-- I didn't hear anything.
But I know they said they're taking us to Tyrol.
Tyrol is-- is in the mountains in Germany or in Austria.
And that's where they're gonna take us.
And so finally, they did on the train.
I don't know you're familiar with Allach, not far from Dachau was also a prison, Allach.
And we stayed one overnight there.
And then I saw an-- an air attack from far.
God, it was a big one, a big one.
And-- and they put us on the trains.
And we were on these trains, going back and forth.
We don't know-- we-- we knew Tyrol, but where and what, we had no idea.
We thought they're gonna dispose of us then.
I saw men while in the trains, when the train stopped and there was another train.
I saw how they were disposing of bodies, so many men, throwing out, you know, dead, dead bodies from the trains.
But in our trains, all of sudden, you know-- you know, who were our guides were the-- the-- the Hungarian Nazis.
There were no more Germans watching us.
And there was a big raid also when we're in the trains, and-- and the Americans, I mean, by the Americans.
The Americans came at that time.
But a day before, a few days before, all of a sudden they're announcing that the Red Cross is coming to us.
No, that's already on the trains, going to Tyrol.
On the train, probably, were-- I don't know, a week, two, maybe two weeks, something like that, back and forth.
And they came, the Red Cross.
And each one of us got a package, you know, the first time I got something there, you know.
And I remember we went down and made a little fire to make milk, to warm up with-- with water.
And we could come out at that time.
And so there was a little hope.
I mean, they-- we already knew something is happening.
And then we went back on the trains, and then was the air raid.
The Americans came, you know, and they had to-- the-- there Germans were still fighting, shooting back, you know.
There was still a fight to catch the train.
And all of a sudden, [LAUGHS] there are the Americans.
And they took out the Nazis.
And I remember all these-- the women, the women especially-- I paid attention to the women because they were such no-goodniks-- and with their hands up.
It was just like God came, you know?
It was something unbelievable, something unbelievable that this happened.
And-- but they took them away and I was angry.
You know, they want-- we wanted more than that, to see-- to see a punishment.
Oh, they took-- oh, I see, they took these women away.
They took all these women away.
And we wanted to see more, you know?
You wanted to see them executed or--
I mean, something that somebody could take, you know, some revenge.
But that's-- well, they were too kind.
The Americans were too kind, too nice.
And then we were taken on-- on buses to the next village.
So they took you off, the Americans took you off the train.
The Americans took us off to a village.
And people got into the houses where the German lived.
And all of a sudden, they said-- they put out signs that they have already so many people that they couldn't have anymore.
Me and my mother and a few others had-- still had to sleep in the-- in the train, which was-- I felt-- I felt it so unfair, you know?
I was-- I was not an aggressive, neither was mother, to just go in and say, hey, you just let me stay in a warm-- in a warm room, you know?
But we didn't do that, and they didn't do it.
They said that, well, it's already occupied, already occupied.
So that made me a little angry.
And then they-- they fed us, you know, with the bread, the-- the white bread, what we weren't accustomed to.
And we waited a couple days, I think.
I was always on the train.
And I was never-- I was never in a-- but they said the next day they're gonna take us to a camp, to a DP camp.
But that was a very sad feeling in my heart.
And then the recollection of the family, the recollection of the family, you know--
I was very, very kind of-- with-- with the happiness, there was sadness.
For once you had a minute to catch your breath and see what's you'd lost.
And then, you know, life went on.
We came to the DP camp.
Feldafing, where I-- we met General Eisenhower there.
Just what an honor, what a beautiful thing to see a general that fought the war and made us free.
It was just a feeling of undescribable feeling, really.
I was so proud, you know, to see him.
It was just something, something to see that.
And I was so happy that I got a picture, a guard, I don't know what the photographer, who he was.
But he said, I got pictures, do you want to buy some?
Now, when he said, do you want to buy some, how did you have money to buy anything?
I don't know what we had.
We had something, something was given to us.
Food and-- and clothes was given to us.
We were-- these used to be for the German soldiers, what you call, the barracks for the German soldiers.
And we were fed a meal every day, cooked meal.
And I still remember that meal.
It was rice with meat, what I would serve to 10 people today, what I would eat myself.
This was-- this was later, a little later what I'm talking.
But at the beginning, they got so sick.
I saw people from the package only what we got from the Red Cross, people would eat up everything and got very, gravely ill and some died.
But me and my mother, we knew how to divide, because I-- that's why we survived, because we took one piece of bread and divide it.
You're not eating more than this one piece, so you have tomorrow another little piece.
And the same thing I did with the package.
It was not too far from Munich.
Some people went there to live.
To visit my friends, because they stayed there.
You know, I mean, they didn't want to stay in-- in--
They didn't want to stay in Feldafing.
So I went to visit them.
We had so many people to talk to and exchange our experiences.
And so-- and then-- and then some people came and told us to get to-- to-- to Switzerland.
They said, Switzerland will let you in because you are refugee.
So my ex-husband, I was there with him and my mother.
We all going there, you know?
So we went first to visit.
And it was a beautiful, beautiful city.
Konstanz and Bodensee on the Kreuzlingen border, Kreuzlingen border, about an hour away from Zurich.
But who would let us in?
The minute I came to Feldafing.
And I was on the truck.
On the truck to go down, he was there.
Oh, he was in Buchenwald and Auschwitz and Poznan and he was in many camps.
But he came into the bunk, to the--
You know, and you know, it happened.
So she was very stoked that he's also from Lodz.
Well, [LAUGHS] it's a lot to talk about.
But I'm not saying-- very, very difficult life.
We went to-- we went to-- we moved into this Konstanz and Bodensee to-- you know, because it was a nice little city.
And we were helped by the UNRRA.
And then we worked a little.
You know, he worked a little.
He was selling, you know, different things.
Oh, he went to those little islands and bought eggs and butter and came back and say, well, always a businessman.
Well, I don't-- we-- just-- at the beginning, you didn't need much.
We got something from the UNRRA.
So you spend, and you make some profit, and that's how people were doing.
Pregnant in-- well, let's see, in '47.
My son was born in-- let's see, November, November in '47.
So I had a baby right there.
In Konstanz, we lived-- a German woman rented a room for us, one room.
Just took care of the baby.
Because our needs were so minimal, really.
I mean, look-- and food we got from the UNRRA.
So-- and he was making a few dollars selling things and buying, yeah.
So it wasn't too bad then, after-- after the war.
The-- the-- the Joint Distribution Committee came over and then said that they can take us.
You know, if we like to go, they will send us to the United States.
And they asked me what city?
I said, it doesn't matter, I said.
I want to be in the United States.
So I wound up in St.
Louis, Missouri, which I didn't like.
I couldn't stand the summers there, you know.
He was still your husband at the time?
And so we bought some ticket, trains-- tickets and came over to the-- to Los Angeles on our own.
From Hamburg, we came over here.
We still-- we still in touch.
But I knew they're coming too.
I knew that-- and a lot of them came already.
You know, the people from that city, what we lived, yeah, we were about 100 of us.
And so a lot I met here, and they're still here.
I came in to New Orleans.
My mother was sent to Detroit, you see.
Because she had a different name, they didn't understand that, you know?
So she was sent to Detroit.
But they helped me to bring her back, you know, to be together.
I-- I couldn't-- I was so nauseous, day and night and day and night, never left me.
He was all right, but he stopped eating.
And when we arrived, he got the measles.
And after the measles, he got pneumonia.
And he had to be taken away from me to the hospital.
